content:
# 1 Corinthians Chapter 13 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1CO_013_page_001.jpg

1. If I speak in the tongues of the men and of the angels, but do not have love, I have become a sounding bronze or a clanging cymbal.

2. And if I have prophecy and know all the mysteries and all the knowledge, and if I have all the faith so as to remove mountains, but do not have love, I am nothing.

3. And if I give away all the possessions of me, and if I deliver the body of me that I may boast, but do not have love, I am profited nothing.

4. The love is patient, the love is kind; it does not envy, it does not boast, it is not puffed up,

5. it does not act improperly, it does not seek the [things] of itself, it is not provoked, it does not reckon the evil,

6. it does not rejoice at the unrighteousness but rejoices with the truth;

7. it bears all [things], believes all [things], hopes all [things], endures all [things].

8. The love never falls. But whether prophecies, they will be abolished; whether tongues, they will cease; whether knowledge, it will be abolished.

9. For from part we know and from part we prophesy;

10. but when the complete comes, the [thing] from part will be abolished.

11. When I was an infant, I was speaking as an infant, I was thinking as an infant, I was reasoning as an infant; when I became a man, I abolished the [things] of the infant.

12. For we see now through a mirror in a riddle, but then face to face. Now I know from part, but then I will know fully just as I was fully known.

13. And now remain faith, hope, love — these three; but the greatest of these [is] the love.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.1 — "tongues of men and of angels":** The Corinthians likely believed their glossolalia was angelic speech. Paul grants the premise hypothetically, then renders it irrelevant without love.

2. **v.3 — "that I may boast" (ἵνα καυχήσωμαι):** Sinaiticus reads καυχήσωμαι ("that I may boast") rather than καυθήσομαι ("that I may be burned"). This is a significant textual variant. The "boast" reading is likely original in Sinaiticus, though "burned" is well attested elsewhere.

3. **v.4 — φυσιοῦται ("puffed up"):** The same word Paul has used throughout the letter for the Corinthian disease (4:6, 18, 19; 5:2; 8:1). Love is the cure for the inflation that has destroyed the community.

4. **v.8 — "Love never falls" (πίπτει):** Not "fails" but "falls" — the image is of something that remains standing when everything else collapses. Prophecy, tongues, knowledge — all temporary. Love is permanent.

5. **v.12 — "through a mirror in a riddle" (δι᾽ ἐσόπτρου ἐν αἰνίγματι):** Ancient mirrors were polished bronze, producing imperfect reflections. ἐν αἰνίγματι echoes Numbers 12:8 (LXX), where God speaks to Moses "not in riddles." The present is a time of partial, indirect knowledge; the future will bring face-to-face encounter.

6. **v.13 — The triad:** Faith, hope, and love — the three enduring realities. "The greatest of these is love" — μείζων, the same word used for "greater gifts" in 12:31. Love is the greatest gift.

— EMET, AI Translator
